---
title: "Unidad 10.2: Reverse Proxy y Balanceo de Carga"
subtitle: "Nginx como gateway para aplicaciones backend"
author: "Diego Saavedra"
date: "2026-03-31"
date-format: medium
---

# Unidad 10.2: Reverse Proxy y Balanceo de Carga

## Introducción

Un reverse proxy es la puerta de entrada estándar en arquitecturas modernas. Nginx recibe las solicitudes y las distribuye a los backend, permitiendo SSL termination, caching, logging centralizado, y balanceo de carga.

## Concepto de Reverse Proxy

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                 REVERSE PROXY EN ACCIÓN                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                  │
│   CLIENTE                 NGINX                   BACKENDS     │
│  ┌────────┐            ┌──────────┐           ┌──────────┐    │
│  │        │  HTTPS :443 │          │  HTTP :3000│ Node.js  │    │
│  │ Navegador│ ────────> │ Nginx    │ ─────────> │ App      │    │
│  │         │            │ (proxy)  │           │          │    │
│  └────────┘            └──────────┘           └──────────┘    │
│                                                                  │
│   + SSL Termination          + Logs centralizados               │
│   + Load Balancing            + Cache de respuestas              │
│   + WAF básico               + Alta disponibilidad             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Configuración de Reverse Proxy

### Proxy Básico

```bash
$ sudo tee /etc/nginx/sites-available/backend-proxy.conf <<'EOF'  # <1>
server {
    listen 80;  # <2>
    server_name api.example.com;  # <3>

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000;  # <4>

        proxy_set_header Host $host;  # <5>
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;

        proxy_connect_timeout 60s;  # <6>
        proxy_send_timeout 60s;
        proxy_read_timeout 60s;
    }
}
EOF

$ sudo ln -sf /etc/nginx/sites-available/backend-proxy.conf /etc/nginx/sites-enabled/  # <7>
$ sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx  # <8>
```

1. **tee**: Writes config file to sites-available in one atomic command.
2. **listen 80**: Listens on HTTP port 80 — HTTPS comes later with SSL termination.
3. **server_name**: Matches incoming requests for this domain.
4. **proxy_pass**: Forwards all requests to the Node.js backend on port 3000.
5. **proxy_set_header**: Passes original request headers so the backend sees real client info.
6. **proxy_connect_timeout**: Limits connection wait to backend — prevents hanging requests.
7. **ln -sf**: Activates the site by symlinking to sites-enabled.
8. **nginx -t && reload**: Validates config syntax before reloading — prevents downtime from bad configs.

### Verificar Funcionamiento

```bash
$ curl http://127.0.0.1:3000  # <1>
{"status": "ok", "version": "1.0.0"}

$ curl -H 'Host: api.example.com' http://127.0.0.1  # <2>
{"status": "ok", "version": "1.0.0"}

$ curl -I -H 'Host: api.example.com' http://127.0.0.1 | grep -i proxy  # <3>
X-Real-IP: 127.0.0.1
X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1
```

1. **Direct curl**: Tests backend directly — confirms it's running before testing proxy.
2. **Via Nginx**: Sends request through proxy with matching Host header — verifies proxy forwarding works.
3. **Check headers**: `-I` shows response headers — confirms proxy headers are being set correctly.

### Headers de Proxy Explicados

| Header | Descripción |
|--------|-------------|
| `Host` | Host original de la request |
| `X-Real-IP` | IP real del cliente |
| `X-Forwarded-For` | IP original (para proxys múltiples) |
| `X-Forwarded-Proto` | Protocolo original (http/https) |
| `X-Forwarded-Host` | Host original |

## Balanceo de Carga

### Upstream Basic

```bash
$ sudo tee /etc/nginx/sites-available/loadbalancer.conf <<'EOF'  # <1>
upstream api_backend {
    server 10.0.1.11:3000;  # <2>
    server 10.0.1.12:3000;
    server 10.0.1.13:3000;
}

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name api.example.com;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://api_backend;  # <3>

        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
    }
}
EOF
```

1. **tee**: Writes load balancer config with upstream definition.
2. **upstream block**: Defines a group of backend servers — Nginx distributes requests across them using round-robin by default.
3. **proxy_pass to upstream**: Routes traffic to the named upstream group instead of a single server.

### Algoritmos de Balanceo

```bash
upstream api_backend {
    ip_hash;  # <1>
    server 10.0.1.11:3000;
    server 10.0.1.12:3000;
    server 10.0.1.13:3000;
}

upstream api_backend {
    least_conn;  # <2>
    server 10.0.1.11:3000;
    server 10.0.1.12:3000;
    server 10.0.1.13:3000;
}

upstream api_backend {
    server 10.0.1.11:3000 weight=5;  # <3>
    server 10.0.1.12:3000 weight=3;
    server 10.0.1.13:3000 weight=2;
}
```

1. **ip_hash**: Sticky sessions — same client IP always hits the same backend. Useful for session state.
2. **least_conn**: Routes to the server with fewest active connections — best for variable request durations.
3. **weight**: Proportional distribution — server 11 gets 50% of traffic, 12 gets 30%, 13 gets 20%.

### Health Checks y Backup

```bash
upstream api_backend {
    server 10.0.1.11:3000 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;  # <1>
    server 10.0.1.12:3000 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
    server 10.0.1.13:3000 backup;  # <2>
    server 10.0.1.14:3000 down;   # <3>
}
```

1. **max_fails + fail_timeout**: Marks server as down after 3 failures in 30 seconds — prevents sending traffic to broken backends.
2. **backup**: Only receives traffic when all primary servers are down — acts as failover.
3. **down**: Permanently removes this server from rotation until manually re-enabled.

## Proxy a Different Backends

### Node.js

```bash
location / {
    proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000;  # <1>
    proxy_http_version 1.1;  # <2>
    proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;  # <3>
    proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade";
    proxy_set_header Host $host;  # <4>
}
```

1. **proxy_pass**: Forwards requests to the Node.js app on port 3000.
2. **proxy_http_version 1.1**: Required for WebSocket and keep-alive connections — HTTP/1.0 won't work.
3. **proxy_set_header Upgrade**: Passes the client's WebSocket upgrade request to the backend — critical for real-time apps.
4. **proxy_set_header Host**: Preserves the original hostname so the backend can route correctly.

```bash
location ~ \.php$ {
    include snippets/fastcgi-php.conf;  # <1>
    fastcgi_pass unix:/run/php/php8.1-fpm.sock;  # <2>
    fastcgi_param SCRIPT_FILENAME $document_root$fastcgi_script_name;  # <3>
}
```

1. **include snippets/fastcgi-php.conf**: Loads standard FastCGI parameters — avoids manual setup of PATH_INFO, SCRIPT_NAME, etc.
2. **fastcgi_pass unix:...**: Communicates with PHP-FPM via Unix socket — faster than TCP for local communication.
3. **SCRIPT_FILENAME**: Tells PHP-FPM which file to execute — the full path to the .php script on disk.

### Python (Gunicorn/uvicorn)

```bash
location / {
    proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8000;  # <1>
    proxy_set_header Host $host;  # <2>
    proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;  # <3>
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;  # <4>
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
    
    # WebSocket support
    proxy_http_version 1.1;  # <5>
    proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
    proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade";
}
```

1. **proxy_pass**: Forwards to Python app on port 8000 (Gunicorn/uvicorn default).
2. **X-Real-IP**: Passes client's real IP to Python — without this, backend sees nginx's 127.0.0.1.
3. **X-Forwarded-For**: Appends full proxy chain — useful if there are multiple proxies upstream.
4. **X-Forwarded-Proto**: Tells backend the client used HTTP or HTTPS — enables secure redirects.
5. **proxy_http_version 1.1**: Required for WebSocket support in Python apps.

### Docker Container

```bash
location / {
    proxy_pass http://172.17.0.2:8080;  # <1>
    proxy_set_header Host $host;  # <2>
    proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
}
```

1. **proxy_pass**: Forwards to a Docker container's internal IP — use `docker inspect` to find the IP.
2. **Host header**: Preserves original hostname so the container can route by virtual host.

## WebSocket Proxy

### Configuración WebSocket

```bash
location /ws/ {
    proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000;  # <1>
    
    # WebSocket headers
    proxy_http_version 1.1;  # <2>
    proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
    proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade";
    
    # Timeouts extendidos para long-lived connections
    proxy_read_timeout 3600s;  # <3>
    proxy_send_timeout 3600s;
    
    # Buffers
    proxy_buffering off;  # <4>
    proxy_request_buffering off;
}
```

1. **location /ws/**: Dedicated path for WebSocket traffic — keeps it separate from regular HTTP.
2. **proxy_http_version 1.1**: Mandatory — WebSocket protocol requires HTTP/1.1.
3. **proxy_read_timeout 3600s**: Keeps idle WebSocket connections alive for 1 hour — default 60s closes them.
4. **proxy_buffering off**: Disables buffering for real-time streaming — messages pass through immediately.

### Verificar WebSocket

```bash
# Test con curl (handshake)
$ curl -i -N \
    -H "Connection: Upgrade" \
    -H "Upgrade: websocket" \
    -H "Sec-WebSocket-Version: 13" \
    -H "Sec-WebSocket-Key: test123==" \
    http://localhost/ws/  # <1>
```

1. **curl -i -N**: Tests WebSocket handshake — `-i` shows response headers, `-N` disables buffering for real-time output.

## Caching con Proxy

### Basic Proxy Cache

```bash
# En http block
proxy_cache_path /var/cache/nginx levels=1:2 keys_zone=api_cache:10M max_size=100M inactive=60m;  # <1>

server {
    location /api/ {
        proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000;
        proxy_cache api_cache;  # <2>
        
        # Cache respuestas 200 por 60 min
        proxy_cache_valid 200 60m;  # <3>
        
        # Keys diferentes por Host
        proxy_cache_key $scheme$host$request_uri;  # <4>
        
        # Agregar header de cache status
        add_header X-Cache-Status $upstream_cache_status;  # <5>
    }
}
```

1. **proxy_cache_path**: Creates cache storage — `levels=1:2` splits dirs, `keys_zone=api_cache:10M` names the cache, `max_size=100M` limits disk, `inactive=60m` evicts unused entries.
2. **proxy_cache api_cache**: Enables caching for this location — uses the named cache from above.
3. **proxy_cache_valid 200 60m**: Caches only successful responses for 60 minutes — errors pass through.
4. **proxy_cache_key**: Cache key includes scheme, host, and URI — so different domains get separate cache entries.
5. **X-Cache-Status**: Shows HIT, MISS, or EXPIRED in response — useful for debugging cache behavior.

### Cache con Purge

```bash
# Location para purgar cache
location ~ /purge(/.*) {
    proxy_cache_purge api_cache $scheme$host$1;  # <1>
    access_log off;  # <2>
}

# Usar: curl -X PURGE https://example.com/purge/api/data
```

1. **proxy_cache_purge**: Removes cached content matching the URI pattern — requires nginx-cache-purge module or nginx 1.9+.
2. **access_log off**: Suppresses logging for purge requests — reduces noise from automated cache invalidation.

## SSL Termination

### HTTPS a HTTP Backend

```bash
server {
    listen 443 ssl http2;  # <1>
    server_name api.example.com;
    
    # SSL certs
    ssl_certificate /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com/fullchain.pem;  # <2>
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/example.com/privkey.pem;
    include /etc/letsencrypt/options-ssl-nginx.conf;  # <3>
    
    # Proxy a backend HTTP
    location / {
        proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000;  # <4>
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto https;  # <5>
    }
}
```

1. **listen 443 ssl http2**: Enables HTTPS with HTTP/2 — modern protocol for faster page loads.
2. **ssl_certificate**: Full certificate chain from Let's Encrypt — `fullchain.pem` includes intermediate certs.
3. **include options-ssl-nginx.conf**: Loads Mozilla's recommended TLS settings — cipher suites, protocols, OCSP stapling.
4. **proxy_pass**: Forwards decrypted traffic to backend on plain HTTP — nginx handles TLS termination.
5. **X-Forwarded-Proto https**: Tells backend the original request was HTTPS — enables secure cookie flags.

## Casos de Estudio

### Caso 1: API con Múltiples Versiones

**Escenario**: API v1 y v2 corriendo en puertos diferentes.

```bash
# API v1
server {
    listen 80;  # <1>
    server_name api.example.com;
    
    location / {
        proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000;  # <2>
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
    }
}

# API v2 (nueva)
server {
    listen 80;
    server_name api-v2.example.com;  # <3>
    
    location / {
        proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:4000;
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
    }
}
```

1. **listen 80**: Both servers listen on HTTP — use separate domains to distinguish versions.
2. **proxy_pass**: v1 routes to port 3000, v2 to port 4000 — different backends per version.
3. **server_name api-v2.example.com**: Separate subdomain for v2 — clients migrate by changing their base URL.

### Caso 2: Redirect según Path

**Escenario**: Diferentes backends según path URL.

```bash
server {
    listen 80;
    server_name example.com;
    
    # Frontend estático
    location / {
        proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8080;  # <1>
    }
    
    # API
    location /api/ {
        proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3000;  # <2>
    }
    
    # Admin panel
    location /admin/ {
        proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:5000;  # <3>
        # Solo desde red local
        allow 10.0.0.0/8;  # <4>
        deny all;
    }
}
```

1. **location /**: Catch-all for frontend — serves the main app UI.
2. **location /api/**: Routes API calls to port 3000 — path-based routing keeps frontend and API on same domain.
3. **location /admin/**: Admin panel on separate port — access-controlled by IP range.
4. **allow 10.0.0.0/8**: Only permits requests from internal network — blocks all external admin access.

### Caso 3: Blue-Green Deployment

**Escenario**: Cambiar tráfico gradualmente entre versiones.

```bash
# Blue (versión actual)
upstream blue {
    server 10.0.1.10:3000;  # <1>
}

# Green (nueva versión)
upstream green {
    server 10.0.1.20:3000;
}

# Por defecto blue
server {
    listen 80;
    server_name example.com;
    
    location / {
        proxy_pass http://blue;  # <2>
        proxy_set_header Host $host;
    }
}
```

1. **upstream blocks**: Define two deployment environments — blue is current, green is the new version.
2. **proxy_pass http://blue**: Routes to the active upstream — switch to `http://green` to deploy the new version.

## Resumen

| Tema | Comando Clave | Propósito |
|------|---------------|-----------|
| Proxy | `proxy_pass http://ip:port` | Forward request |
| Headers | `proxy_set_header` | Pass headers al backend |
| Upstream | `upstream name {}` | Grupo de backends |
| Balanceo | `ip_hash` / `least_conn` | Algoritmo de distribución |
| WebSocket | `Upgrade $http_upgrade` | WebSocket support |
| Cache | `proxy_cache` | Cachear respuestas |

## Referencias

- [Nginx Reverse Proxy](https://docs.nginx.com/nginx/admin-guide/web-server/reverse-proxy/)
- [Nginx Load Balancing](https://docs.nginx.com/nginx/admin-guide/load-balancer/http-load-balancer/)
- [Nginx Proxy Module](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/ngx_http_proxy_module.html)
